suggestions: State the core data-shape convention once at the catalog level (feature tables and distance matrices are both long), since nearly every function depends on it and an agent reading only the catalog blurb won't infer it.
Name the schema namespaces explicitly (skbio.sequence / diversity / stats / tree) so an agent can map a task to a schema without expanding each one.
Mention the input domains supported (DNA/RNA/protein sequences; long feature tables; long distance matrices) so an agent can decide relevance before drilling in.
0d81ef77b60b5d1c.summary: Accurate, well-scoped catalog blurb; would benefit from surfacing the long-format data convention and schema names up front.

suggestions: Note that alpha counts are rounded to integers and NULLs skipped (a real caveat that lives on the individual aggregates but matters when choosing this schema).
State beta_diversity's default metric (braycurtis) and that it emits a full square matrix including the zero diagonal, so downstream grouping/ordination expectations are clear.
The doc_llm references `skbio.stats.pcoa`/`permanova` but the schema is `skbio.stats` — keep the qualified names consistent to avoid an agent constructing a wrong function path.
b92903797dd71fbf.summary: Strong, accurate schema summary listing all seven alpha metrics and the beta table function; minor caveat/units gaps.

suggestions: hamming_distance is described as 'pairwise' — clarify it's a two-column scalar over equal-length sequences (and what happens on length mismatch) so an agent knows it's not an all-pairs matrix.
translate omits the genetic-code/frame and stop-codon behavior; a one-clause note would let an agent use it correctly without opening the function.
Spell out that composition table functions return long `(sequence, token, count)` rows (as diversity does), rather than a wide token matrix, since output shape drives query construction.
b7370fd52e170969.summary: Accurate and complete function inventory; a few per-function semantics (hamming shape, translate frame) are under-specified for direct use.

suggestions: The grouped tests (permanova/anosim) need a 4th `group` column and mantel needs two distance columns — flag these differing input shapes here so an agent picks the right query layout before drilling in.
Note that pcoa/clr/ilr are table functions with fixed-at-bind output width (e.g. pcoa n_components) versus the single-row permanova/anosim/mantel test outputs.
Mention distance inputs are symmetrized and zero-diagonal-filled from the long form, so an agent can safely pass a triangle or a full square.
a450d7070f2d592a.summary: Accurate multivariate-stats summary; the divergent input shapes across permanova/anosim/mantel deserve a schema-level callout.

suggestions: State that neighbor_joining is a table function consuming the long `(id_1, id_2, distance)` matrix and returns a single Newick string, versus the two scalars that take Newick per row — the shape contrast is the key selection cue.
Note the tree is unrooted and that NJ requires a complete distance matrix (as beta_diversity's full-square output provides).
Add units for total_branch_length (sum of branch lengths in the tree's distance units) so an agent interprets the scalar correctly.
2333d951bd529b37.summary: Accurate and concise; would gain from stating input/output shapes and the unrooted/complete-matrix caveats.

suggestions: The metric list ends with '...'; enumerate the full supported set (also chebyshev, correlation, sqeuclidean) or state that unsupported metrics raise at bind, so an agent doesn't guess an invalid name.
Clarify that counts for duplicate (sample, feature) rows are summed, not overwritten, since the long input may contain repeats.
Note that id_1/id_2 are emitted as VARCHAR (sample ids stringified) even when the source ids are integers, which affects downstream joins for permanova grouping.
c364315ca6bf0bfa.summary: Excellent, thorough docs matching the code — full-square long output, defaults, pivoting, and downstream hookup all covered.

suggestions: State the units/base explicitly (nats, natural log) and typical range so an agent can sanity-check or convert to log2 bits if needed.
Note the behavior for a single-feature or empty sample (returns 0 / NULL) so agents handle edge cases.
The declared param type is DOUBLE but counts are rounded to integers internally — mention that fractional abundances are rounded, not truncated, to avoid surprise.
b5d694b9290c57e3.summary: Accurate, complete aggregate doc with clear GROUP BY guidance and integer-rounding/NULL caveats.

suggestions: Explicitly relate dominance = 1 − simpson (and inv_simpson = 1/dominance) so an agent can choose among the related metrics without computing it.
Note the single-feature edge case yields dominance 1 (max), the informative boundary of the [0,1] range.
As with shannon, flag that the DOUBLE count input is rounded to integers, since dominance is defined on abundances.
e67edbafb3ca73e6.summary: Accurate and complete; range, interpretation, and Simpson relationship are all clearly stated.

suggestions: State behavior for degenerate groups (single feature or all-NULL/empty group) — does it return 0, NULL, or error?
Cross-reference inv_simpson (= 1/(1-simpson)) so an agent can pick the right dominance-family metric.
Sharpen the parameter doc: 'Abundance value' → 'one feature's count within the grouped sample'; only the multiset of counts matters, not feature identity.
00e5406883bde524.summary: Accurate and clear Simpson doc; add edge-case behavior and a metric cross-reference.

suggestions: Clarify that zero counts (not just NULLs) are excluded from richness, since the long table may carry explicit 0 cells.
Note that integer rounding is irrelevant here (richness ignores magnitude) so agents don't worry about count scaling.
Mention this is the unadjusted baseline that chao1 corrects, to guide metric selection.
692c79ba521c445d.summary: Correct richness doc; spell out zero-count handling and its relation to chao1.

suggestions: Document the no-singletons/doubletons case (chao1 collapses toward observed richness) so agents interpret unexpectedly low estimates.
State that non-integer inputs are rounded before singleton/doubleton counting, which can change the estimate — warn against pre-normalized/relative abundances.
Note the output can be non-integer (a DOUBLE estimate) and is >= observed_features.
7101373d0c60e2d3.summary: Solid chao1 doc; add degenerate-case behavior and the rounding caveat for relative abundances.

suggestions: Give the output range [1, richness] — unlike the other metrics its doc omits bounds.
State the exact relationship inv_simpson = 1/(1-simpson) so agents don't compute both redundantly.
Note the 'effective number of features' interpretation makes it comparable across samples of differing richness, unlike raw simpson.
00fdf5132cf93272.summary: Accurate inverse-Simpson doc; add its numeric range and explicit tie to simpson.

suggestions: Document the single-feature edge case where the normalizer log(richness)=0 (evenness is undefined — clarify whether it returns NULL/NaN).
Note it is unitless and independent of Shannon's log base since the base cancels in normalization.
Mention pairing with observed_features/Shannon since evenness alone doesn't convey richness.
211e4410f680f785.summary: Clear Pielou doc; add the undefined single-feature case and log-base independence.

suggestions: Give the `residue` column type (single-character VARCHAR) and state that row/residue ordering is unspecified.
Document handling of invalid/lowercase/gap characters and NULL sequences — are those rows skipped, error, or counted?
Clarify the emitted `id` column's type and that it mirrors the input id column (only present when id := is supplied).
fb0bbf1e6ce72731.summary: Good long-format residue-count doc; specify residue type, ordering, and invalid-input handling.

suggestions: State behavior when k exceeds a sequence's length (no k-mers → no rows) and require k >= 1.
Clarify 'overlapping' with a concrete count example (e.g. 'AA' in 'AAA' = 2) and give the `kmer` column type.
Note that pivoting to a wide feature matrix yields a sparse/ragged vocabulary across sequences (missing k-mers = 0), which the caller must fill.
b3ec16d72d94ec73.summary: Strong k-mer doc; add k-vs-length edge case, an overlap example, and pivot sparsity note.

suggestions: State the empty/all-gap sequence case (0/0 denominator) — does it return NULL or 0?
Clarify whether IUPAC degenerate bases (S, N, etc.) count toward GC or invalidate the sequence to NULL.
Note it is DNA-only (not RNA) so agents don't pass RNA and silently get NULL.
b15c3b32678ad15c.summary: Excellent, precise GC-content doc; only edge cases (empty seq, degenerate bases, RNA) remain.

suggestions: State that the output VARCHAR has the same length as the input (a useful invariant for agents building alignments).
The parameter `seq` description ('A DNA sequence') is thinner than the doc_llm; note case-insensitivity and NULL/non-DNA→NULL there too so agents reading only param metadata get it.
Clarify how mixed-case/whitespace is normalized in the returned string (uppercased? stripped?) since doc says whitespace is ignored on input but not what the output casing is.
62f084f239cbe61d.summary: Accurate and agent-friendly; only missing the length-preservation invariant and richer param-level docs.

suggestions: The example `'ATGCGGATTACAGG'` has no T-run edge case beyond single Ts; add an all-uppercase and a lowercase example to demonstrate the case-insensitivity claim.
Note whether RNA input (already containing U) is accepted or treated as non-DNA→NULL, since users may chain from other RNA-producing steps.
Enrich the `seq` parameter description to mention the T→U rule and NULL behavior, matching the doc bodies.
566011c9e518c4ef.summary: Clear and correct DNA→RNA doc; edge-case handling of existing-U/RNA input is unspecified.

suggestions: Spell out that comparison is position-wise and case-insensitive but NOT gap-aware (gaps count as ordinary characters), so agents don't assume alignment semantics.
Confirm the zero-length edge case: what does comparing two empty strings return (0.0 or NULL)?
In the `seq2` param, note that a length mismatch yields NULL rather than an error, mirroring the doc_llm.
fe380bb0eafe6d9a.summary: Excellent: units, range, NULL/length rules, and the raw-count conversion are all covered.

suggestions: Explicitly note that the empty string and whitespace-only strings — state whether these are valid (true) or false, since predicates are often applied to dirty data.
Pair the doc with `is_valid_protein`'s caveat: mention that a valid-DNA string is also valid protein, so this predicate is the correct one for DNA-specific gating.
List or link the exact accepted character set (including which gap chars: `-` and/or `.`) so agents can predict results without trial.
14dc6bcb91d32adc.summary: Accurate predicate doc; empty-string behavior and the exact gap-character set are left implicit.

suggestions: Great DNA-overlap caveat in doc_llm — also surface it in doc_md, which omits it.
State whether stop `*` and selenocysteine/pyrrolysine (U/O) are accepted, since these appear in real protein data and affect validity.
Clarify empty-string and gap-character handling explicitly, as for is_valid_dna.
c3966c5daad1c340.summary: Strong, with a valuable DNA/protein-overlap note; ambiguity around `*`/U/O and empty input remains.

suggestions: Add a one-line note that biological convention rarely uses the un-reversed complement, steering agents to reverse_complement unless they specifically want same-order — the cross-reference is present but the 'when to use this' framing could be sharper.
State length preservation (output length == input length).
Enrich the `seq` param description to mention case-insensitivity and NULL/non-DNA behavior.
7e3ecaba1786adfa.summary: Precise and well-contrasted against reverse_complement; only lacks a 'when would you want this' nudge.

suggestions: State that only reading frame +1 (from base 0) is supported and there is no frame/strand argument, so agents needing other frames must offset the input themselves.

Clarify whether translation stops at the first stop codon or emits `*` and continues through the whole sequence (the example `'ATGCGGATTACAGGT'` doesn't hit a stop, so behavior is unillustrated) — add an example containing a stop codon.
Note the genetic-code table is fixed (NCBI table 1) with no code-selection parameter, and that input is DNA not RNA (contrast with transcribe).
6d749c38476bad2a.summary: Accurate standard-code translation; single-frame limitation and stop-codon continuation behavior deserve an explicit example.

suggestions: doc_md lists a `method` output column but doc_llm omits it — reconcile so the full output schema (method, test_statistic, p_value, sample_size, number_of_groups, permutations) is stated identically in both.
The `permutations` parameter description omits its default (999) that appears in the prose; put the default in the param metadata too, and note p_value resolution is bounded by 1/(permutations+1).

Call out the critical constraint from the design notes: every sample must appear as `id_1` (full-square beta_diversity guarantees this; a condensed triangle leaves the last sample ungrouped and errors) — agents building the input need this to avoid failures.
25cf466d36e01533.summary: Thorough test doc with a good worked example; output-schema mismatch between docs and the id_1-coverage constraint should be surfaced.

suggestions: State the axis cap: PCoA cannot return more axes than samples, so requesting n_components > sample_count NULL-pads the extra pc_k columns — an agent parsing pc_3 as always-numeric will otherwise break.
Note that the input must cover a full/symmetric distance matrix (the diagonal is zero-filled and pairs symmetrized), and mention the minimum of ~3 samples.
Add that pc_k values have arbitrary sign/orientation (eigenvector direction is not fixed) so cross-run signs may flip, and clarify each pc_i is a DOUBLE.
9276180d7246566f.summary: Accurate and clear, but omits the sample-count axis cap and NULL-padding behavior an agent needs.

suggestions: Document the minimum-size / degenerate-input behavior (Mantel needs ≥3 ids; too few permutations or identical matrices affect the p-value) so agents don't feed 2-pair inputs.
Clarify that distance_x and distance_y must be aligned on the SAME id pairs and that both are symmetrized/zero-diagonal-filled like other distance inputs.

Note the p_value is one-sided by default and give the type of each output (correlation/p_value DOUBLE, n/permutations BIGINT); parameter_types ordering also puts BIGINT out of sync with the listed permutations param.
81e6aa2d81d3f825.summary: Solid coverage of columns and methods; missing alignment/minimum-size caveats and p-value tail direction.

suggestions: Warn that ILR `component` indices are basis-dependent and NOT individually interpretable or comparable to feature ids — unlike CLR — so agents shouldn't map component→feature.
State the D≥2 minimum (a 1-feature sample yields zero components) and that missing (sample,feature) cells are treated as 0 before the transform.
Explain the interaction of pseudocount with zeros vs. the 'values must be positive' rule (without a pseudocount, any zero/negative errors), and that `value` is declared VARCHAR but must be numeric.
039fc6ef70571901.summary: Correctly distinguishes ILR from CLR but lacks the key caveat that components are uninterpretable basis coordinates.

suggestions: Add the defining property that CLR values sum to zero per sample (singular covariance) — the reason ILR exists — so agents know not to use CLR output where full rank is required.
Note that features absent for a sample (sparse long input) are zero-filled and thus require a pseudocount, and that the output is one row per (sample, feature) present after pivoting.
Clarify `value` is typed VARCHAR but must parse as a positive number, and specify the output `clr` column is DOUBLE.
b9200b3e722449fb.summary: Accurate and clear; would benefit from the sum-to-zero caveat and explicit zero-fill/pseudocount interaction.

suggestions: State the hard requirement that EVERY sample must appear as id_1 (a full-square beta_diversity matrix guarantees this; a condensed triangle leaves the last sample ungrouped and errors) — the single most likely failure mode.
Note that `group` must be consistent per id_1 across its rows and that ≥2 groups are required, plus the effect of very small groups on the R/p-value.
Give output column types (test_statistic/p_value DOUBLE, sample_size/number_of_groups/permutations BIGINT) and note R<0 is possible (within > between).
0b09c633825616bf.summary: Statistically clear with a good example, but omits the every-sample-as-id_1 constraint that governs valid input.

suggestions: Warn that NJ can produce negative branch lengths and requires ≥3 taxa; also that the tree is unrooted so the returned Newick has an arbitrary root placement.
Note the input must be a full/symmetric distance matrix (diagonal zero-filled) and that duplicate/inconsistent id pairs are symmetrized.
Mention how tip labels map to id_1/id_2 values so agents can reliably join tip_count/total_branch_length results back to samples.
d9a9c9777663c26c.summary: Clear and accurate, but should flag negative branch lengths, the ≥3-taxon minimum, and unrooted-root arbitrariness.

suggestions: Add a one-line note on what counts as a tip (leaf/taxon labels, not internal nodes) for trees with named internal nodes.
State that the actual output column is named `result` (scalar convention) so agents alias it when needed.
Optionally note behavior on a single-node/empty Newick (e.g. '();') to remove ambiguity.
d5e155e88cad0289.summary: Concise, accurate, and well-suited to agents; only minor edge-case notes missing.

suggestions: Soften/qualify the Faith's PD claim: it only equals Faith's PD for a rooted tree spanning a single sample's present features — for a neighbor_joining beta-diversity tree it is simply total edge weight, which could mislead an agent into misusing it.
Note that NJ trees may contain negative branch lengths, so the sum can be less than a naive expectation or even negative.
State the output column is named `result` (DOUBLE) and that unit is whatever the input distances/branch lengths are in.
0318cc086b6a7e1f.summary: Clear and mostly accurate, but the Faith's PD equivalence is overstated for general (e.g. NJ) trees.

suggestions: Name the concrete alpha metrics available (shannon, simpson, inv_simpson, observed_features, chao1, pielou_evenness, dominance) so an agent can pick one without opening the function list.

State beta_diversity's output columns (id_1, id_2, distance), its default metric (Bray-Curtis), and that it emits a full square where every sample appears as id_1 — the property group tests downstream depend on.
Note the input caveat that alpha metrics operate on integer abundances (counts are rounded), which affects chao1 and richness-style results.
69d5d3246375af44.summary: Accurate and well-shaped, but omits the concrete metric names and the beta output-column/units contract an agent needs.

suggestions: "compare reads to a reference" overstates hamming_distance, which is a position-by-position count over two equal-length sequences (no alignment); reword to "Hamming distance between two equal-length sequences" and note the equal-length requirement.

Clarify that k-mer/residue composition are table functions over a long (id, sequence) input (buffering) while the rest are row-wise scalars returning a single `result` column — the two shapes are used very differently.
Spell out what 'validate' returns (boolean is_valid_dna/is_valid_protein) and that transcribe/translate assume DNA input, so an agent doesn't guess the alphabet direction.
61bcd75d751985a7.summary: Clear and mostly accurate; the 'compare to a reference' phrasing misrepresents the equal-length pairwise Hamming function.

suggestions: Surface that the three hypothesis tests do NOT all take the same input: permanova/anosim need a 4th grouping column (id_1, id_2, distance, group) built by joining a grouping table onto beta_diversity, while mantel takes two distance columns (id_1, id_2, distance_x, distance_y) — the single biggest usage gotcha here.

Distinguish clr vs ilr and note they consume a long (sample, feature, value) feature table (not a distance matrix), pivoted internally with missing cells = 0 — the shape contrast with ordination/tests is easy to miss.
Mention pcoa's n_components arg and that it cannot return more axes than samples (extra requested columns are NULL-padded).
43c7a8e75d420e91.summary: Accurate at a high level but hides that permanova/anosim/mantel and clr/ilr each require different input shapes — critical for an agent building the query.

suggestions: Note that neighbor_joining is a table function returning a single Newick string (one row/one value), whereas tip_count/total_branch_length are row-wise scalars over a Newick VARCHAR column — the call syntax differs.
State that total_branch_length is in the same distance units as the input matrix, and whether malformed Newick degrades to NULL or errors, so an agent knows the failure mode.
Add one worked example of the full chain (beta_diversity → neighbor_joining) since the doc already asserts the input comes from beta-diversity.
9fb612df26390504.summary: Accurate and appropriately scoped; would benefit from the scalar-vs-table call distinction and branch-length units.

suggestions: Split the doc_llm mega-sentence into per-schema clauses; as one ~120-word run-on it is hard for an agent to scan and match a task to a schema.

Name the actual schema namespaces an agent must qualify against (e.g. `skbio.alignment`, `skbio.diversity`, `skbio.stats`, `skbio.tree`); the doc_md's five prose groupings ('Sequence' + four others) don't obviously map to the schema list, and CLAUDE.md still says 'four schemas'.

State the two core data-shape conventions up front (feature tables are long `(sample,feature,count)`; distance matrices are long `(id_1,id_2,distance)`), since nearly every table function depends on them.
5011e57c96eee9c8.summary: Accurate and comprehensive catalog blurb, but the doc_llm is a single overloaded sentence and never names the schemas an agent routes to.

suggestions: List the four member functions by name (pairwise_align_nucleotide/protein, align_score_nucleotide/protein) so an agent can pick score-only vs full-alignment without introspecting each object.

State that scoring parameters (substitution matrix, match/mismatch, gap penalties) are fixed defaults and not exposed as arguments — an agent tuning alignment sensitivity will otherwise hunt for knobs that don't exist.
Define 'aligned length' (length of the gapped alignment, not of either input) since the schema promises 'aligned strings and score' but the functions also emit a length column.
eb86a818c09183c3.summary: Clear and accurate schema summary that omits function names and the fixed-scoring caveat.

suggestions: Name the exact output columns of the beta-diversity matrix (`id_1, id_2, distance`, full square) so the reader can see it feeds directly into skbio.stats and skbio.tree without a reshape.
Note that alpha metrics operate on integer abundances (counts rounded in finalize) — a caller passing relative frequencies or normalized values should know they'll be truncated.
Specify rarefaction's depth/seed behavior (samples below depth dropped? deterministic seed?) since 'rarefy each sample to a common depth' hides the two decisions a user most needs to control.
91613a4216843cce.summary: Strong, well-scoped diversity overview; light on output-column specifics and the integer-count and rarefaction caveats.

suggestions: Make the input-shape split explicit per method: PCoA/PERMANOVA/ANOSIM/Mantel consume the long distance matrix, while PCA/CA and the log-ratio/ANCOM methods consume the long feature table — an agent otherwise can't tell which functions need a beta_diversity result vs a raw table.

For the log-ratio transforms, flag that ILR/ALR need a basis/reference (ALR a denominator feature) and that CLR/ILR require nonzero (pseudocount-adjusted) compositions — the current 'into real space' prose omits the zero-handling gotcha.
Name the differential-abundance outputs and grouping input (ANCOM's per-feature W statistic; how the group label is supplied) so a caller knows what a result row contains.
28d6f34d4457cb4b.summary: Accurate multivariate summary, but per-method input requirements and compositional zero/reference caveats are underspecified for tool selection.

suggestions: Add the `length` output to the one-line `description` and `doc_md` header (both say only 'aligned strings and score' while Returns is `(id?, aligned_1, aligned_2, score, length)`).

Document the output column names/types explicitly (aligned_1/aligned_2 VARCHAR, score DOUBLE, length INTEGER) and what `length` measures (gapped-alignment length), since table functions fix their schema at bind and callers need the exact names.

Clarify default column detection when `id :=` is omitted: state whether the first column is still treated as id, and note the top-level `comment` is empty so all guidance lives only in doc_llm/doc_md.
2bb7bf814498d91e.summary: Solid usage doc whose short description omits the emitted `length` column and the exact output schema.

suggestions: Surface the `length` output column in the `description` and `doc_md` header, which currently advertise only 'aligned strings and score' despite the `(id?, aligned_1, aligned_2, score, length)` return shape.
Spell out the output column types and define `length` (length of the gapped alignment) so a downstream query can bind columns without trial and error.

Note that nucleotide scoring (match/mismatch, affine gaps) is fixed and not configurable, and that inputs are upper-cased/validated as DNA — a caller aligning RNA or IUPAC-ambiguous reads should know they may collapse to NULL.
28efece2182324c9.summary: Clear alignment doc that names N-W/S-W modes but under-reports the `length` output and fixed scoring.

suggestions: Give the concrete default scoring values (match, mismatch, gap-open/extend) instead of 'default nucleotide scoring' so an agent can interpret the DOUBLE magnitude rather than treating it as opaque.

Warn that the raw score is unnormalized and length-dependent, so it is not comparable across pairs of differing length — an agent ranking similarity should normalize or use a percent-identity approach.
State that the score is symmetric in its two arguments, so callers needn't compute both orderings.
c7497dc1b1330624.summary: Accurate, clear scalar-score doc; would benefit from concrete scoring values and a non-normalization caveat.

suggestions: Name the actual default substitution matrix (e.g. BLOSUM50/62) and gap penalties rather than 'the default substitution matrix', since matrix choice materially changes the score an agent will reason about.
Note the score is unnormalized and length-dependent, hence not directly comparable across protein pairs of different lengths — pair with alignment length or percent identity for ranking.
Add a worked expected value to the example (like a returned score) so a caller can sanity-check integration, and mention the score is symmetric.
20d793b986903f11.summary: Accurate and clear protein-score doc; vague on which substitution matrix/penalties define the returned DOUBLE.

suggestions: State the distance range (0–1) and whether the emitted long matrix includes self-pairs (id_1 == id_2, distance 0) and both orderings, since downstream pcoa/permanova via distance_utils expects every sample as id_1.
Note that weighted vs unweighted UniFrac are not comparable on the same scale, and that unweighted ignores the count column entirely (presence/absence).
Add a caveat about feature ids not present as tree tips (error vs silently dropped), mirroring faith_pd's tree-membership note.
437c7fee53640753.summary: Accurate and well-targeted; missing distance range and self-pair/orientation details of the long output.

suggestions: Clarify behavior when `seed :=` is omitted (nondeterministic each run) so agents know reproducibility requires an explicit seed.
Specify the type/units of `depth` (integer total counts per sample) and what happens if a sample's total exactly equals depth.
Mention that features dropping to zero after subsampling are omitted from output rows, and that the output count column is integer.
d8c56f9cadd697d2.summary: Strong rarefaction docs; would benefit from seed-default and edge-case (equal-depth, zeroed features) notes.

suggestions: State explicitly that faith_pd uses presence/absence only — the count column value is ignored beyond nonzero — so agents don't assume abundance-weighting.
Give the units of the result (sum of branch lengths, in the tree's branch-length units) and note it is unbounded above.
Clarify whether samples with no matching tips return 0 or NULL, complementing the 'missing feature raises an error' note.
4d226a7c7e9b4a17.summary: Accurate PD description; should note presence/absence semantics and result units.

suggestions: Tighten the definition: Simpson's evenness is (1/D)/S where D is dominance and S is richness; 'inverse-Simpson divided by richness' is right but 'inverse-Simpson diversity' is ambiguous — name it.
The boilerplate 'values are rounded to integers' is misleading for evenness — clarify it means the input counts are rounded to integer abundances, not the DOUBLE result.
Note interpretation: near 1 = perfectly even, near 0 = dominated, and behavior for a single feature (S=1).
eec7ef6ba8bc365d.summary: Clear but the evenness formula wording and the shared 'rounded to integers' caveat need disambiguation.

suggestions: Emphasize that this counts singletons after integer rounding of counts, so fractional/relative-abundance inputs will be rounded first (can change the singleton count materially).
Clarify the returned type is a whole number represented as DOUBLE, and that it is a raw richness component (input to chao1), not a normalized diversity index.
Note the [0, S] range and that it is scale-sensitive (unlike evenness), so it should not be compared across samples of different sequencing depth without rarefaction.
f1f46b09fe0debb8.summary: Accurate singleton-count docs; rounding sensitivity and depth-comparability caveats would help agents.

suggestions: Refine wording: D is the probability two randomly drawn individuals belong to the SAME feature ('share a feature' is fine but easily read as 'differ'); explicitly note it is Σ p_i², in [0, 1], higher = more dominated.
Disambiguate from `simpson` (which is 1 − D) so an agent picking between them knows they are complementary.
Clarify the 'rounded to integers' note applies to input counts, not the DOUBLE result.
a64da28f2143fc72.summary: Mostly accurate dominance index; sharpen the same/different wording and contrast with simpson.

suggestions: Cross-reference that this equals 1 − simpson_d (Gini–Simpson index) so agents don't compute both redundantly.
Clarify that 'rounded to integers' refers to input abundances, not the [0,1] DOUBLE output.
Add interpretation anchors: 0 = single dominant feature, approaching 1 = many equally abundant features.
909e559d12b09baf.summary: Accurate Gini–Simpson description; add explicit relationship to simpson_d and interpretation anchors.

suggestions: Verify the log base: scikit-bio's shannon defaults to base 2 (bits), not natural log — the doc's 'natural log' claim is likely inaccurate and should state the actual base used (and whether it is configurable here).
Give the value range/units (0 to log(S); units depend on base) so agents can interpret magnitudes.
Clarify that 'rounded to integers' applies to input counts, and note that raw Shannon is depth-sensitive so cross-sample comparison benefits from rarefaction.
2119847e2201a8ae.summary: Clear but the stated 'natural log' base likely contradicts scikit-bio's base-2 default — an accuracy risk.

suggestions: Define 'singletons'/'doubletons' concretely as the number of features whose (rounded) count equals 1 and 2 respectively — an agent can't map [observed, singles, doubles] to inputs otherwise.
The example groups by sample but never shows the array shape in its output; add a comment or expected result like [3,1,1] for sample 1 so the DOUBLE[] return is unambiguous.
State the intended use (feeding richness estimators like chao1) and that it is the only metric here returning an array, not a scalar, so callers unnest or index it.
e387023ec8d51378.summary: Accurate array-return doc but under-defines singleton/doubleton semantics and never illustrates the array output.

suggestions: The `q` parameter exists and is used in the example (q := 1) but is undocumented in both doc_llm and doc_md — describe it, give its default, and note it is a scalar constant not a column.

The prose says 'entropy of order q' yet the count-rounding caveat contradicts an entropy computed on proportions; clarify whether q must be >= 0 and what q=0/1/2 yield (the parameter description hints richness/Shannon/Simpson but the doc body omits it).
Explain how `q` is passed (named arg only? positional after count?) since the example uses `q :=` — agents need the calling convention.
a05984cc04a814c9.summary: Documents the metric but omits its required `q` parameter entirely, the key accuracy/completeness gap.

suggestions: Note edge behavior: when there are no doubletons the bias-corrected form changes/can inflate — agents should know results are unstable for tiny samples.
Clarify the DOUBLE input + 'rounded to integers' interaction: chao1 depends on integer singleton/doubleton counts, so fractional abundances round first and may collapse to 0.
State the return range (>= observed richness) so an agent can sanity-check outputs.
c5b0e7f7ef3e5efb.summary: Correct and clear; missing small-sample caveats and the meaning of the rounding step for singleton-driven math.

suggestions: Document the single-feature edge case: Heip's evenness divides by (richness - 1), so one observed feature yields division-by-zero/NaN — agents need this before grouping sparse samples.
Clarify that '[0,1]' is the nominal range but degenerate inputs can fall outside/return NULL.
Contrast briefly with the richness metrics (why pick evenness vs observed_features) to aid selection.
b543203df327f560.summary: Accurate range-bounded description but omits the richness=1 divide-by-zero caveat.

suggestions: Flag that 'rounded to integers' happens before the non-zero test, so an abundance of 0.4 rounds to 0 and is NOT counted — surprising given DOUBLE input.
Note the result is always a whole number even though the return type is DOUBLE.
Mention it equals the `observed` element of `osd`, helping agents avoid redundant calls.
8b497ca396d34eb6.summary: Clear richness definition, but the round-then-count interaction can silently drop small fractional abundances.

suggestions: Give the exact formula S/sqrt(N) with S=richness, N=total count so agents can verify and compare with margalef.
Note the total-count sensitivity: unlike observed_features it depends on sampling depth, so cross-sample comparison assumes comparable depth (or prior rarefaction).
State behavior when total count is 0 (empty/all-NULL group).
22a5b15b85153729.summary: Accurate one-line index description; lacks the depth-dependence caveat that governs correct use.

suggestions: State the range (>= 1, up to richness) so agents can interpret and validate the 'effective number' framing.
Note it is depth-robust relative to raw richness metrics — useful for selecting it over observed_features/menhinick.
Clarify empty/single-feature behavior (returns 1 for a single feature).
de487ad3203a92b5.summary: Strong, accurate 'effective number' framing; missing value range and selection guidance.

suggestions: The formula is (S-1)/ln(N), not 'richness scaled by natural log'; correct the prose to include the S-1 numerator and the division by ln(N).
Document the N=1 (or single-feature) edge case where ln(N)=0 gives division-by-zero/NaN.
Note total-count dependence so agents know it, like menhinick, assumes comparable sampling depth across groups.
570d587d0449a51b.summary: Slightly imprecise formula wording (drops the S-1 / division) and omits the ln(N)=0 edge case.

suggestions: Clarify that 'values are rounded to integers' refers to the input abundances (per the integer-count requirement), not the returned DOUBLE — the current placement next to 'Returns a single DOUBLE' reads as output rounding.
Note the degenerate-input behavior: Q is undefined/NaN when a sample has too few distinct features to span the interquartile range, so agents know when a NULL/NaN can appear.
State the expected magnitude/units (slope in units of features per log-abundance) and that higher Q means greater diversity, so an agent can interpret the number.
2fdbb39a26ca83b1.summary: Accurate and clear, but omits units/interpretation and edge-case (too-few-features) behavior.

suggestions: doc_md omits the `q` parameter entirely from its bullet list even though the example passes `q := 1` — document q as a named argument, its type, and whether it has a default.
State whether q is required or optional and its default value; the doc_llm describes q semantically but never says how to supply it.
Clarify that the result is an effective feature count (same units as richness), so q=0 returns the observed feature count for the example rows.
53beb72f61056b7c.summary: Good metric explanation, but the required `q` argument is under-documented and missing from doc_md.

suggestions: State the interpretation direction (0 = perfectly even abundances, 1 = maximal inequality/dominance) so an agent can act on the value.
Clarify that 'rounded to integers' applies to input counts, not the [0,1] output.
Note behavior for a single-feature sample (Gini = 0), a common edge case.
f6f3411bd768869a.summary: Accurate, well-bounded [0,1] description; add directionality and single-feature edge case.

suggestions: Note that Good's coverage depends on singletons (features with count 1) and returns 1.0 when none exist — critical for interpreting the integer-rounding step, since rounding fractional values can erase or create singletons.
State the output range [0,1] and its meaning (proportion observed vs. undiscovered = 1 − coverage).

a6f14a4c5fd0bdc0.summary: Accurate but silent on singleton dependence and how integer-rounding affects the estimate.

suggestions: Note the estimator's dependence on singleton count and total individuals (Robbins = singletons/(N+1)), so agents understand why integer-rounding of inputs matters.
State the output range [0,1] and that it estimates unseen-feature probability (complement of coverage).
Clarify that 'rounded to integers' applies to the input `count`, not the DOUBLE result.

suggestions: Add interpretation direction (higher = more dominance/less even) and the [0,1] range so the number is actionable.

suggestions: State the output range and direction (DW in [0,1], higher = more dominance) so the value is interpretable.

suggestions: Flag the sharp edge that counts are rounded to integers before computing — if an agent passes relative/proportional abundances (0–1), every value rounds to 0 and the result is meaningless; state that raw integer counts are required.

d194cb5005bd3105.summary: Accurate and clear; missing the integer-rounding pitfall for relative-abundance inputs and edge-case behavior.

suggestions: State the output range/units: enspie is an effective number of species in [1, richness], not a bounded [0,1] index — helps an agent interpret the magnitude.

suggestions: State the range (1/richness to 1) and that dominance = 1/enspie, so an agent can pick the right sibling metric.

suggestions: Document whether `q` has a default and is optional; the example passes `q := 1` but neither doc states the default, which an agent needs to call it without guessing.

e0b7821b2e1fcb1c.summary: Prose is fine but the `q` default/requiredness, q=1 limit, and output units are underspecified.

suggestions: Note that evenness is undefined (NaN, since ln(richness)=0) for a single-feature sample, and state what the aggregate returns there.

67410a705cef5681.summary: Solid scalar doc; add units/normalization and mismatched-taxa behavior.

suggestions: State explicitly that the sum spans every edge including internal branches (it traverses all nodes), not just terminal/tip branches, so agents don't mistake it for a tips-only sum.

Tighten the Faith's-PD claim: it equals Faith's PD only when the per-row tree is already pruned to exactly the sample's features; note that the SQL builders here (nj/upgma/...) produce such a tree, but an arbitrary reference tree will overcount.
Note that branch-length units are whatever the tree encodes (arbitrary/unitless) and that negative branch lengths (possible from nj/gme/bme) are summed as-is, so results can be smaller than expected.
09f18bf97fed98e6.summary: Accurate and clear; only the Faith's-PD equivalence and the 'all edges' scope need slight sharpening.

suggestions: Clarify it counts leaf nodes only (excludes internal/root nodes), so agents don't expect a total node count.
Mention edge behavior for a single-taxon/degenerate Newick (e.g. 'a;') and that a repeated/duplicate tip label still counts once per leaf node.
Add a second example threading a builder's output (e.g. tip_count over a neighbor_joining result) to show the intended pipeline.
84eae88e2d71a35e.summary: Correct and concise; a touch sparse on leaf-only semantics and edge cases.

suggestions: Warn that mismatched taxon sets do NOT error or return NULL — skbio's compare_rfd silently returns a misleading value (verified: {a,b,c,d} vs {a,b,c,e} → 0.0), so agents must ensure identical tip labels themselves.
Explain the value is always a whole number despite the DOUBLE return type, and that it is unnormalized (max = 2*(n-3) for n taxa); suggest dividing by that for a comparable [0,1] score.

Note that RF requires resolved/bifurcating comparisons behave differently under polytomies, and that it ignores branch lengths (already said) so weighted_robinson_foulds is the branch-aware alternative — cross-link it.
6888a404e0ae8c51.summary: Definition is accurate but omits the dangerous silent-value-on-mismatched-taxa behavior and normalization guidance.

suggestions: Add the key caveat that height is rooting-dependent: nj/gme/bme produce unrooted trees whose parsed 'root' is arbitrary, so height is only meaningful for rooted/ultrametric trees (upgma) — an agent could otherwise apply it to an nj tree and get a meaningless number.
Clarify that missing branch lengths count as 0, so a topology-only Newick yields height 0 (or an undercount when some lengths are present), rather than NULL.
State units are tree-dependent/unitless, matching the branch-length scale of the input.
332f216b54fb2e08.summary: Formula is accurately described but the crucial rooting-dependence caveat for unrooted builder trees is missing.

suggestions: Correct the metric description: skbio's default is metric='unitcorr' = (1-r)/2, a unit correlation distance bounded in [0,1] — NOT the raw 'how much the path-length matrices disagree' Euclidean magnitude the docs imply; fix both doc_llm and doc_md.

Fix the misleading 'branch-length sensitive' claim: because it is correlation-based it is scale-invariant (verified: two proportionally-scaled trees give exactly 0.0), so it reflects relative structure, not absolute branch lengths; say '0 = identical up to scale, 1 = maximally uncorrelated'.
Document behavior on non-identical taxon sets (it compares over shared tips) and state the fixed [0,1] range so agents can interpret magnitudes.
6804d0b414b4bc6b.summary: Mischaracterizes the default unit-correlation metric as an unbounded scale-sensitive path-length distance; it is actually a [0,1] scale-invariant measure.
